references.text: [Gu04] Guy, Richard K., Unsolved problems in number theory. (2004), xviii+437.

remarks.text:
Carmichael has asked whether there is an integer $t$ for which $\phi(n)=t$ has exactly one solution. Erd\H{o}s has proved that if such a $t$ exists then there must be infinitely many such $t$.

See also [694].

This is discussed in problems B36 and B39 of Guy's collection \cite{Gu04}.

statement.text: Is there an infinite set $A\subset \mathbb{N}$ such that for every $a\in A$ there is an integer $n$ such that $\phi(n)=a$, and yet if $n_a$ is the smallest such integer then $n_a/a\to \infty$ as $a\to\infty$?
